The bet assumes AI coding tools replace institutional knowledge. They don't, they depend on it. Fire the engineers who carried the why behind the code, and you get plausible suggestions on top of a context model nobody owns. The decay shows up later as compounding incoherence.

Generation scaled, review didn't. Mandating AI tools without scaling human attention to vet the output isn't productivity, it's hidden technical debt. The artifacts pile up, decisions get made on stuff no one read, and the consequences arrive on a delay.

The original clause was a profit threshold, around $100B cumulative, not a capability test. Killing it is a renegotiation of upside, not a quiet admission about AGI timelines. The capability rhetoric is what they're saying. Profits are what they're trading.

Line chart titled “GitHub’s Historic Uptime.” A note below says all data is sourced from the official status page. The “Average” tab is selected, with a “Breakdown” tab beside it.

The chart shows GitHub average uptime by month from April 2016 to January 2026, excluding Codespaces and Copilot. Uptime stays nearly flat at 100% from 2016 through late 2019. A vertical dashed line around January 2019 marks “Microsoft Acquires GitHub.” After 2020, monthly uptime varies more, mostly between about 99.8% and 100%, with several dips. The deepest visible dip occurs around mid-2023, slightly above 99.5%. The final point near January 2026 drops to roughly 99.66%. Green, yellow, and red markers indicate higher to lower uptime performance.

record.text:
The era of "unlimited AI for $20/month" is over. GitHub Copilot adjusted its multipliers. Claude Code was momentarily pulled out of Pro plans.

The bills are arriving.

Running frontier models is expensive, someone was going to pay eventually. Those who'll feel it least know what they use and why.

Two things worry me about developers and AI.

The ones on the sidelines, waiting for things to settle. They won't.

And the ones moving so fast they've stopped understanding what they build.

Neither ends well.

The ones who'll thrive stay curious, keep sharp, and never stop owning what they ship.

I get the fear. I really do.

Every time technology shifts this fast, something feels at stake. Jobs. Craft. Identity.

But every abstraction we gained freed up space for harder, more interesting problems.

AI is that shift. Faster, yes. Scarier, maybe.

I'd rather meet it with curiosity than fear.

images.alt:
"A male red‑bellied woodpecker bursts away from a wooden bird feeder in a moment frozen between power and grace. His wings are flung wide, each feather edged in crisp black-and-white bars that blur slightly with the speed of his lift‑off. The sunlight catches the bright red crown and nape on his head, making the color glow like a small flame against his pale, creamy face and chest.

Clutched firmly in his beak is a whole peanut, angled forward as if he’s determined not to lose his prize. His body is stretched and streamlined, tail feathers fanned just enough to steady his flight.

Behind him, the background melts into soft green foliage—no sharp shapes, just a wash of color that makes the bird’s details stand out even more. The feeder sits to the side, weathered and simple, a quiet stage the woodpecker has just launched himself from. The entire scene feels like a split-second of wild energy, captured before the bird disappears into the trees." - Microsoft Copilot

record.text: Copilot in OneNote now understands more of your notes

external.description: In Microsoft OneNote, you can use Copilot to reason over your notes to summarize content, extract key insights, and build on your ideas. We’ve improved the way Copilot responds to your queries in OneNote, making it easier to interact with your notes using natural, everyday language. Copilot in OneNote now understands images, tables, and more Copilot in OneNote can now better understand your notes beyond typed text—including note tags, images, and tables—to deliver more accurate responses to your queries with the additional context. This adds to the types of rich content Copilot can already read over, such as inked notes: OneNote Copilot now supports inked notes | Microsoft Community Hub. By reasoning over the richer set of information captured on the page, Copilot can provide better responses that understand the context in your notes. This enables a more conversational experience, where you can continue asking follow-up questions or exploring ideas without needing to reframe your request or ask a different way to incorporate the content in your other note types. How it works You don’t need to do anything new. Simply ask Copilot questions about your OneNote page, which can include note tags, images, tables, inked notes and more. Copilot will be able to understand these note types and give you more accurate responses to your questions. Here are some examples. Before, Copilot did not differentiate between checked off to-do items in your notebook pages: It did not recognize tables nor images either: Now, Copilot understands and reasons over the full range of content in your notebook, allowing it to answer questions with deeper insight. Scenarios to try If you’re planning an upcoming trip and your notebook page includes a table outlining your itinerary alongside images such as destination photos or screenshots of reservations, you can ask: "Based on the itinerary and reservation details on this page, are there any activities or bookings I may have missed for this trip?" Availability This feature is rolling out to users with a Microsoft 365 Copilot (Premium or Basic) license and running: OneNote on Windows: Version 2601 (Build 19628.20128) or later. OneNote on Mac and iOS: Version 16.106 (Build 26020821) or later. Feedback We want to hear from you! Please submit your comments and feedback by selecting Help > Feedback in OneNote or by using the Copilot thumbs up or down buttons to send comments on a specific Copilot response.
external.title: Copilot in OneNote now understands more of your notes

Silo-Bench's 1,620 experiments: agents form coordination topologies fine, exchange information fine, then systematically fail to synthesize distributed state into correct answers. The bottleneck isn't communication. It's reasoning over what's been communicated.

MAST annotated 1,600 multi-agent traces across seven frameworks. Failure rates land between 41% and 87%. The top three: step repetition, reasoning-action mismatch, agents unaware of termination conditions. None of those are model capability problems. They're system design problems.

Auf rotem Hintergrund stehen übergroße gelbe Buchstaben, die das Wort „REPUBLIK“ bilden. Jeder Buchstabe wird von einer anderen Person mit einer Stange hochgehalten. images.alt:
»Kamui-san Directly Behind You« entsteht bei Zero-G × ZG-R unter der Regie von Takumi Tsukumo, die Series Composition übernimmt Motofumi Nakajō, das Character Design stammt von Toshinari Yamashita (Divergence Eve), das Sub Character Design übernimmt Nana Hiiragi, die Chief Animation Direction liegt bei Takayuki Noguchi und Tomoyuki Abe, die Soundregie übernehmen Shūhei Abe und Nankaiteiō und die Musik komponiert Chihiro Endō.

📜 Beschreibung 》
Shizuka ist eine normale Oberschülerin, die Geister sehen kann und sie magisch anzuziehen scheint. Weil das allein ziemlich gefährlich ist, arbeitet sie als Assistentin für den berühmten Spirituellen Kamui. Der ist zwar extrem selbstsicher, doch seine Methode, Geister auszutreiben, ist alles andere als gewöhnlich. Zusammen geraten die beiden immer wieder in bizarre, übernatürliche Situationen.

images.alt:
📜 Beschreibung 》
Der einzige Lichtblick im tristen Arbeitsalltag des mittelalten Sasaki ist das freundliche Lächeln von Yamada, einer Kassiererin im 24-Stunden-Supermarkt, den er regelmäßig besucht. Doch eines Abends verpasst er ihre Schicht und trifft hinter dem Laden auf die provokante und geheimnisvolle Tayama, die ihn kurzerhand zum gemeinsamen Rauchen einlädt. Zwischen neckischen Kommentaren und Zigarettenpausen entsteht eine unerwartete Nähe, während Sasaki nun aus gleich zwei Gründen im Supermarkt auftaucht. Mit jeder Begegnung zeigt sich, dass hinter Tayamas spielerischem Auftreten mehr steckt, als es zunächst scheint. Eine leise, erwachsene Geschichte über Einsamkeit, kleine Fluchten und unerwartete Verbindungen beginnt.

external.description: The story centers on the Wilbur J. Cohen Federal Building (formerly the Social Security Administration headquarters) in Washington, D.C., a 1940 New Deal-era structure on Independence Avenue near the National Mall. It houses a significant collection of New Deal murals and artworks, particularly a large-scale series by artist Ben Shahn titled The Meaning of Social Security (1942), along with works by Philip Guston, Seymour Fogel, and others.  The Murals Shahn's paired murals (roughly 70 feet long each) depict the pre-Social Security hardships of the Great Depression—poverty, unemployment, old age, and child suffering—on one side, contrasted with post-Social Security scenes of community, labor, family security, public works, and abundance on the other. They were commissioned through the Treasury Section of Fine Arts and painted directly onto the plaster walls (fresco-secco technique), making them extremely difficult and costly to remove without damage. Other pieces include Guston's Reconstruction and the Wellbeing of the Family and friezes on the building's exterior. Preservationists have called the ensemble the "Sistine Chapel of the New Deal" for its artistic quality, historical significance, and thematic celebration of Social Security as a response to economic despair.  The Controversy In 2025–2026, the General Services Administration (GSA)—which manages federal real estate—placed the building on its list of properties for "accelerated disposition" (fast-tracked sale or transfer) as part of broader efforts to reduce the federal government's real estate footprint, cut maintenance costs, and "right-size" holdings. The building had housed Voice of America offices (after the SSA moved), and with VOA facing cuts or relocation under the Trump administration (including input from the Department of Government Efficiency, or DOGE), it became vacant and targeted for disposal.  Critics, including art historians (e.g., from Living New Deal), preservationists, Democratic lawmakers, and advocacy groups like Social Security Works, argue this risks demolition by a private buyer who would prioritize development value over historic art. Since the murals are integral to the walls, demolition would likely destroy them. A retired GSA preservation official alleged the administration was soliciting bids to bypass normal reviews and accelerate demolition of this and three other D.C. federal buildings to sell the land (potentially to developers). Reports also mentioned concerns over weakened historic preservation rules (e.g., Section 106 reviews).  Opponents frame it symbolically as an "attack on Social Security" itself—erasing visual history tied to the New Deal program amid broader debates over the program's future, workforce/staffing changes at SSA, and efficiency reforms. Petitions (e.g., at stopthedemolition.org or Change.org) and op-eds in outlets like The New Republic, NYT, The Atlantic, and Hyperallergic have mobilized to save it.  Context and Counterpoints GSA's perspective: This fits longstanding federal efforts to shed underused properties for fiscal reasons. GSA has denied plans for outright illegal demolition and pointed to standard processes. The building is historic but not uniquely occupied by core SSA functions today (SSA headquarters operations are elsewhere, such as in the Baltimore area). Trump administration angle: Trump and allies (including DOGE efforts led by figures like Elon Musk) have prioritized government efficiency, cutting waste, reducing bureaucracy, and rethinking New Deal-era expansions. Selling surplus D.C. real estate aligns with reducing the federal footprint. There's no public statement from Trump personally calling for "destroying the murals" as an end in itself; the issue arose from property disposition lists. Critics link it rhetorically to other SSA-related reforms (staff reductions, field office changes, etc.), but those are separate operational matters. Practical realities: Federal buildings get sold or repurposed routinely. Removing integrated frescoes is technically challenging. Similar New Deal art has faced threats before (e.g., during the Cold War over political themes), but much survives.  The narrative gained traction in left-leaning media and advocacy circles as cultural vandalism or symbolic hostility to Social Security, while efficiency advocates see it as routine asset management of an aging, underutilized structure. As of early 2026 reporting, the building remains at risk pending sales processes, legal challenges, or preservation interventions. No murals have been destroyed yet. The core tension is fiscal/practical priorities versus historic/artistic preservation.
external.title: What’s the story with Trump wanting to destroy the Social Security building and the murals it contains?

正直言って、今のLogicoolはオワコンに近い状態だと思っている。
UnifyingからLogi Boltに移行し始めた辺りからおかしくなりだした。
正直言って企業向け対応なんて完全放置で良かったと思うし、太客(企業)を切り捨てたくなかったんなら別のシリーズに切り分けるべきだった。
あと、昔のLogicoolは加水分解せずに寿命まで使い続けられたので、今のLogicoolは明らかにわざと加水分解させてるんじゃないかと思う。自分はこれをLogitimerと呼んでいる。

以前はLogicool信者だったおいらでも今のLogicoolの製品は耐えられないので、今ではGeminiがおすすめしてくれたKeychronのマウスで大変満足しています。

Keychron M3A1

電池はバッテリー式(USB充電)
LED発光あり＋色変更可能
電池が無いから軽い
加水分解でベトベトしない
センサー部がマウス中央なのでマウス操作で変な癖が付かない

値段は高いけれど。Logicoolの同価格帯と比べれば遜色ない機能がある。
素晴らしいです。
